title: Pyrography leaf coaster
subtitle: Burned oak-leaf design on a sycamore coaster blank
excerpt: A single oak leaf burned onto a 90 mm round sycamore coaster blank, finished with a brushed walnut oil. A first pyrography project that introduces the writing tip, the shading tip, and the design transfer step. Strict allowed-wood and ventilation rules apply.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III on pokerwork (the Victorian name for pyrography). Period craft manuals on wood-burning safety and allowed timbers.

content.text: A 90 mm round coaster with a single oak leaf burned onto it. The work is in two passes: outline with the
writing tip
first, then shading with the
shading tip
to bring the leaf forward against the pale sycamore background. A first
pyrography
project. The leaf is the standard subject for learning the relationship between tip temperature, dwell time, and burn depth.

Ventilation: open a window minimum, run an extractor fan ideal. Wood smoke is a respiratory irritant; some woods release toxic fumes (yew, oleander, treated lumber, never use these). Stick to the allowed-woods list below.
Heat: the burner unit stays hot for fifteen minutes after switch-off. Park it on a dedicated metal stand, not a bench surface.
Eye protection: safety glasses when grinding or shaping tips.
Fume sensitivity: stop and ventilate the room if your throat feels scratchy.

Allowed pyrography woods: birch, lime/basswood, sycamore, maple, beech, poplar, untreated pine. Never burn: yew, oleander, walnut (some sensitivity), treated lumber of any kind, MDF, plywood (adhesive fumes).
The wood

A 90 mm by 8 mm round sycamore blank. Sycamore is the recommended species: pale, fine-grained, burns to an even brown, no oily resins to flare or stain. Coaster blanks come from craft suppliers or are cut from any pale sycamore offcut with a hole saw in a cordless drill. Sand the blank smooth on both faces with 240 grit; any tooth in the surface shows in the finished burn.
Tools
A solid-tip pyrography burner with a writing tip and a shading tip, the dedicated cooling stand, and walnut oil for the finish. Carbon paper or a soft pencil for the
design transfer
. Note the walnut-oil allergen flag: if a recipient has a tree-nut allergy, swap to raw linseed oil for the same effect with a longer cure.
Workspace

A well-lit bench near an open window or under an extractor. The cooling stand sits at the off-hand side of the work, within easy reach. A small bowl of water and a clean cloth nearby for cooling the tip between sessions if you change tips mid-work.
Method

Draw the oak leaf on paper at full size. Five lobes either side of a central vein, 80 mm tall and 50 mm wide, with the stem at the bottom. Trace a single oak leaf from a real leaf or copy any clear botanical drawing. Keep the design simple at this stage; an elaborate leaf is harder to burn cleanly.

Transfer the design to the blank. Two methods: lay carbon paper between the drawing and the blank, then trace the outline firmly with a hard pencil. Or: shade the back of the drawing heavily with a soft pencil, place it face up on the blank, and trace the outline with the hard pencil. Both leave a faint pencil outline on the wood.

Fit the writing tip. The solid-tip burner needs to be cold when tips are changed: switch off, wait 15 minutes, replace the tip, switch on. Allow another 5 minutes for the new tip to reach working temperature.

Test the heat on an offcut. Touch the tip briefly to the wood. A pause of 1 second leaves a clear brown line; longer leaves a deeper black mark. Adjust technique to suit; with a solid-tip burner the only control is dwell time and stroke speed.

Burn the outline of the leaf following the pencil line. Hold the tip like a pencil. Draw the outline in one continuous motion where possible; lifts and restarts show as darker dots if the tip lingers. Work the central vein next, then the side veins outward.
Switch off the burner and wait 15 minutes. Swap the writing tip for the shading tip. Switch back on and allow 5 minutes to come to heat.

Shade the leaf. Hold the shading tip flat to the wood surface and use long sweeping strokes, working from the central vein outwards to each lobe edge. Build colour gradually: two light passes are smoother than one heavy pass. Let the heat fade into the lobe tips by lifting the tip earlier in each stroke.

Add detail to the central vein. Hold the shading tip on edge for a sharper line; run it along the vein outline to deepen the contrast and give the leaf the slight three-dimensional feel a real leaf has.
Switch off the burner and park it on the cooling stand. The work cools at room temperature for 30 minutes before finishing.
Finishing

Apply walnut oil with a soft cloth: wipe on, let soak in for an hour, wipe off the surplus. Walnut darkens the sycamore slightly and deepens the contrast against the burned leaf. Cure for 2 weeks before placing under a hot drink. Cork or felt feet glued to the underside protect the finish from a wet base on the coaster.
Variations
Set of four: repeat the design on four blanks. Burning four at one sitting gives the most consistent shading; the eye and the tip-heat both calibrate over the first one or two.
Different leaves: maple, ash, hawthorn. The method is the same; the lobed-leaf shape suits the round coaster better than long thin leaves like willow.
Birch instead of sycamore: birch burns slightly darker than sycamore at the same dwell time. Adjust the dwell on a test piece before starting the design.
Care
Wipe with a damp cloth after use, dry immediately. Do not soak. Re-oil with a single coat of walnut once a year; the cured oil lasts longer than that but a refresh deepens the colour.

items.fix: Practice landing and lifting the tip while in motion; the tip should be moving when it touches the wood and still moving when it leaves. Test on an offcut before returning to the work.
items.cause: Tip lingered for a moment at the start or end of each stroke
items.symptom: Outline has dark dots where strokes started or stopped

items.fix: Work in longer continuous strokes, keep the angle of the tip constant, allow heat to recover between strokes. Build colour in light passes; do not try to reach final tone in one pass.
items.cause: Tip temperature varied between strokes, or strokes overlapped unevenly
items.symptom: Shading is patchy with light and dark areas

items.fix: Wipe the tip on a damp cloth when cool. Check the wood: if it is pine and produces resin streaks under heat, swap to sycamore or birch.
items.cause: Wood contains resin (pine offcut instead of sycamore) or tip is fouled with carbon from earlier work
items.symptom: Tip leaves a sticky residue on the wood

items.fix: Stop immediately. Ventilate the room. Check the wood: if it is treated, MDF, or one of the prohibited species, dispose of it. Resume only when air is clear.
items.cause: Wood is wrong species, or ventilation is inadequate
